---
title: "Usage"
---

## Installation

```julia
using Pkg
Pkg.add(url = "https://github.com/fesmc/PlasticIceSheet.jl")
```

The core package depends only on `LinearAlgebra`. Reverse-mode differentiation and NetCDF
I/O are loaded on demand through package extensions (below).

## Solving

```julia
using PlasticIceSheet

z_s, H = solve(z_b, τ_b, mask; dx, dy, mode = :full)
```

| argument | meaning |
|---|---|
| `z_b`  | bed elevation $z_b$ (m), `nx × ny` matrix |
| `τ_b`    | basal shear stress (Pa); a scalar is broadcast to a uniform field |
| `mask` | `true` where grounded ice exists; the margin is the mask boundary |
| `dx`, `dy` | grid spacings (m) |

Key options (see the docstring for the rest):

- `mode` — `:full` (default, full Hamilton–Jacobi over arbitrary relief) or `:flat`
  (flat-bed eikonal, cheapest; exact when relief ≪ thickness).
- `params` — a `PlasticParams` (densities `ρ_i`/`ρ_w`, gravity `g`, sea level `z_ss`,
  `H_min`).
- `n_outer`, `outer_tol`, `relax` — `:full` damped fixed-point controls. `relax` ∈ (0,1] is
  the under-relaxation factor (default 0.5); lower is more robust on steep beds but slower.

Returns `(z_s, H)` — surface elevation $z_s$ and thickness $H$; ice-free cells have
`H = 0`, `z_s = z_b`.

### Diagnostics

```julia
ice_volume(H, dx, dy)                # m³
ice_area(mask, dx, dy)               # m²
surface_misfit(z_s, z_s_obs, mask)   # mean-squared surface misfit over ice
```

## Velocity and surface mass balance

The plastic solve returns only geometry. A **DIVA closure** layered on the fixed geometry
recovers a depth-averaged velocity and a first-order SMB — see
[Algorithms §8](algorithms.qmd#sec-closures) for the theory. Pick a rheology and a sliding
law:

```julia
rheology = GlenRheology(A = 1.0e-16, n = 3.0)           # isothermal Glen
sliding  = WeertmanSliding(β = 1.0e4, m = 3.0)          # also LinearSliding(β),
                                                        # RegularizedCoulomb(C, u_0, m)
```

### Closure B — velocity, then implied SMB

```julia
vel = diva_velocity(z_s, H, τ_b, mask, dx, dy; rheology, sliding)
# named tuple: ux, uy, speed, u_b (sliding), u_def (deformation), ex, ey (flow direction)

smb = smb_from_velocity(vel, H, z_s, mask, dx, dy)      # ḃ = ∇·(ūH)
```

`RegularizedCoulomb` returns a `NaN` basal speed wherever `τ_b ≥ C` (bed at the Coulomb
cap, sliding speed undetermined) — those cells should take their speed from closure A.

### Closure A — balance velocity from a prescribed SMB

```julia
bal = balance_velocity(z_s, H, mask, smb, dx, dy)       # named tuple: speed, ux, uy, Q, ...
```

`balance_flux` (routing) is the exact inverse of `smb_from_velocity`, so feeding closure B's
SMB back through closure A recovers closure B's velocity to machine precision.

### Consistency — the implied bed

```julia
ib = implied_basal_velocity(z_s, H, τ_b, mask, smb, dx, dy; rheology)
# u_basal = u_balance − u_def: the basal velocity the geometry + SMB demand
```

Inverting a sliding law at `ib.u_basal` and the local `τ_b` gives the friction implied by
the reconstruction — a check on whether the geometry and assumed climate are consistent with
a plausible bed.

### SMB-driven reconstruction

The dual of `solve`: drive the geometry with an SMB instead of a yield stress, on the same
fixed margin (see [Algorithms §9](algorithms.qmd#sec-reconstruct)).

```julia
z_s, H = diva_reconstruct(z_b, mask, smb, dx, dy; rheology, sliding)
```

::: {.callout-warning}
The outer iteration is non-contractive (like `:full`) and is under-relaxed; the default
`relax = 0.05` converges the test configurations, but stiffer or finer grids need a smaller
`relax` and more `n_outer`. A complete free-margin steady state is out of scope — use a
dynamic model (e.g. [Yelmo](https://github.com/palma-ice/yelmo)) for that.
:::

## Inverting for $\tau_b$

The solver is differentiable with respect to $\tau_b$. See [Algorithms §7](algorithms.qmd#sec-ad)
for the theory.

### Forward mode — scalar / low-dimensional $\tau_b$

```julia
using ForwardDiff
loss(τ_b) = surface_misfit(first(solve(z_b, τ_b, mask; dx, dy)), z_s_obs, mask)
g = ForwardDiff.derivative(loss, 1.0e5)
```

### Reverse mode — full per-cell $\tau_b$ field

Load the extension triggers, then differentiate `differentiable_thickness` with any
reverse-mode AD:

```julia
using ImplicitDifferentiation, ADTypes, ForwardDiff, Zygote

loss(τ_b) = surface_misfit(differentiable_thickness(τ_b, z_b, mask; dx, dy) .+ z_b, z_s_obs, mask)
g = Zygote.gradient(loss, τ_b)[1]      # gradient w.r.t. the whole τ_b field
```

A complete, runnable Adam-based recovery of a spatially-varying $\tau_b$ from an observed
surface is in
[`examples/invert_tau.jl`](https://github.com/fesmc/PlasticIceSheet.jl/blob/main/examples/invert_tau.jl).
It drives the misfit down by four orders of magnitude and recovers the two-region truth to
within a fraction of a kPa.

### Choosing a parameterization

How you parameterize $\tau_b$ — a regularized per-cell field, or a few bed-derived tiles —
matters as much as the gradient. The real-data Greenland example
[`examples/invert_taub_greenland.jl`](https://github.com/fesmc/PlasticIceSheet.jl/blob/main/examples/invert_taub_greenland.jl)
implements both and switches with one env var:

```bash
julia --project=examples examples/invert_taub_greenland.jl                  # tiled (default)
METHOD=percell julia --project=examples examples/invert_taub_greenland.jl   # point-by-point
```

See [Inversion](inversion.qmd) for the parameterizations, the link to ICESHEET's hand-tuned
shear-stress domains, and a resolution test for the number of tiles.

::: {.callout-tip}
The default reverse-mode linear solver is direct (robust for modest grids). For very large
fields, pass an iterative solver — see the `differentiable_thickness` docstring.
:::

## NetCDF I/O

```julia
using NCDatasets

inp = load_plastic_inputs("inputs.nc")             # (; z_b, τ_b, mask, dx, dy, x, y)
z_s, H = solve(inp.z_b, inp.τ_b, inp.mask; inp.dx, inp.dy)
save_reconstruction("out.nc", z_s, H; x = inp.x, y = inp.y)
```

## Caveats

- Steady-state geometry only — biased thick/peaked where the real ice was streaming or
  dynamic (low effective driving stress the plastic assumption cannot see).
- $\tau_b$ carries essentially all the uncertainty. For a first guess a constant
  (~50–100 kPa) or a bed-keyed value is fine; do not over-interpret the result as a
  constrained reconstruction.
- Very steep beds (relief approaching the ice thickness over a few cells) may not converge
  in `:full` — see [Algorithms §3](algorithms.qmd#sec-full).
